explicit_text: My family lived on the outskirts of Utsunomiya City, which has since become part of the city proper. I was born there in 1897 and spent my early childhood in that rural environment. Although my family was once well-to-do, we faced financial difficulties which led me to leave Utsunomiya at the age of 12 to seek work in Tokyo. It was a small farming area, quite different from the big cities I later experienced.
implicit_text: My family lived on the outskirts of a city in Tochigi Prefecture, which has since become part of the city proper. I was born there in 1897 and spent my early childhood in that rural environment. Although my family was once well-to-do, we faced financial difficulties which led me to leave that area at the age of 12 to seek work in the capital. It was a small farming area, quite different from the big cities I later experienced.

explicit_text: I moved to Tokyo when I was about 12 years old to work as an apprentice boy because my family was struggling financially. In Tokyo, I first worked at a tabi maker's home and later at a pastry shop, including Kimuraya bakery near Keio University. I lived there through significant events like the death of Emperor Meiji and the Great Kantō earthquake in 1923. Tokyo was where I spent my youth and early working years before eventually coming to America.

explicit_text: While I was working as an apprentice in Tokyo, I became acquainted with a relative's son who was attending Waseda University. He was boarding at the tabi maker's home where I worked and helped me find a job as a helper. Although I did not attend Waseda myself, this connection was important in starting my apprenticeship in Tokyo.

explicit_text: I worked at Kimuraya bakery in Mita, near Keio University, when I was older and could remember that time well. My duties included odd jobs and delivering bread to customers. It was one of the places I worked before I joined the steamship crew and eventually came to America. Kimuraya was a significant part of my early working life in Tokyo.

explicit_text: I vividly remember when Emperor Meiji died in August while I was in Mita, Tokyo, near the Imperial Palace. People gathered at the Imperial Plaza at night carrying paper lanterns to pray for his recovery, creating a solemn and memorable scene. After his funeral, General Nogi and his wife committed suicide, which was widely reported and left a strong impression on me.

explicit_text: I remember the day after Emperor Meiji's funeral when General Nogi and his wife committed suicide. The news was everywhere, and newspapers showed the blood-stained floor mat from his room. This tragic event was part of the national mourning I witnessed while living in Tokyo near the Imperial Palace.

explicit_text: I worked as a baker on board a steamship of the Nihon Yusen Company, thanks to a chief engineer who was a customer I knew from my bakery days. I spent quite a long time on their ships, including during World War I. The company’s ships traveled routes from Yokohama to San Francisco and Europe, and I enjoyed the life at sea and visiting foreign ports.

I spent quite a long time on their ships, including during the First World War. The company’s ships traveled routes from Yokohama to San Francisco and Europe, and I enjoyed the life at sea and visiting foreign ports.

explicit_text: During World War I, I was working on board a Nihon Yusen steamship as a baker. Because of the war, we could not use the Suez Canal and had to go around the Cape of Good Hope. German submarines were a threat, so our fleet was guarded by a destroyer. It was a tense but memorable time at sea.

It was a tense but memorable time at sea.

explicit_text: During World War I, the Suez Canal was closed to our ships, so we had to sail around the Cape of Good Hope instead. This made the journey longer and more dangerous because of German submarines in the area. I experienced this route while working on a Nihon Yusen steamship.

explicit_text: Yokohama was the main port where our Nihon Yusen steamship departed and returned from. We stopped there after trips to places like Kobe, Nagasaki, Moji, and Shanghai. While docked in Yokohama, the crew took turns having days off and enjoyed the city. It was an important hub in my life at sea.

explicit_text: Shanghai was the first foreign port I saw while working on the ship. I was fascinated by the Chinese coolies with their braided pigtails. We stopped there on the way to Europe, and it was an exciting place for me as a young man seeing the world for the first time.

explicit_text: London was the last stop on our European route before returning to Yokohama. When I first landed there, some church ladies showed us around the city, and I saw the tombs of kings and queens in a cathedral. It was a remarkable experience for me, as unlike many other Issei, I had seen the world before coming to America.

explicit_text: The Korea Maru was the ship I last traveled on when I came to America in 1920. It was a Nihon Yusen steamship that carried many third-class passengers and picture brides. I became acquainted with some of these passengers and was invited to come to America, which is how I decided to immigrate.

explicit_text: San Francisco was my first American port of arrival in 1920. As a crew member on the ship, I could not disembark there because the crew were not allowed to land, fearing they might run away. Later, I lived in San Francisco after immigrating and was interviewed there in 1977. It became my home on the West Coast.
